title: The Macro Minute: Are asset markets sleepwalking into a turbulent summer?

Happy Friday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Friday May 30th
2025. As always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or just to get on the right side of
market risk in case you haven't been
following kissing Dr. Mo need to be a
client of 42 macro. So today's key macro
question is are asset markets
sleepwalking into a turbulent summer?
Our answer potentially, but as long as
paradigm C remains the highest
probability outcome, and we still think
it is, as supported by the April PCA
report, investors must buy the dip in
anticipation of a potentially explosive
multi-quarter move higher in risk
assets. That delta would be driven by
persistent upside capitulation by the
large swath of investor consensus that
still needs to transition from being
myopically focused on tariffs to
understanding the full distribution of
probable economic and policy outcomes
amid our paradigm C framework. There is
simply not enough time between the end
of the reciprocal tariff delays and the
2026 midterm election cycle for the
Trump administration to go back to
pursuing paradigm B. As such, investors
must continue to treat tariff headlines
and congressional brinksmanship
regarding the one big beautiful bill act
as scarecrows to be faded over the
medium term. So essentially what I'm
asking you guys here is to
relax. We we're probably going to have a
turbulent summer. There's a lot of stuff
that's unresolved with respect to trade
policy, with respect to the one big ugly
bill, you know, but I'm wearing my, you
know, my floral island breeze cool shirt
man today just to give you a very clear
signal of how you should be approaching
this summer as an investor. Cool, calm,
relaxed, chill, good vibes, and most
importantly, dispassionately executing
our kiss and Dr. those signals. I don't
know how those signals are going to
evolve over the summer with all these
potential scarecrows to risk manage in
the medium term, but I think I have a
very high probability scenario that
they're going to be very b ragingly
bullish by some point in the second half
of this year heading into what could
potentially be an explosive up move
towards the end of the year or well into
2026. Investor consensus is
underposition for paradigm C. We can see
it in our positioning model. I talk to
institutional investors every single day
of the week. We're advising on $25
trillion plus of institutional client
AUM and to a person no one is fully
invested for paradigm C. Everyone's got
one foot in in this puddle and another
foot in that puddle. You know, it's
trying to divide the difference between
paradigm B and paradigm C. And we're
telling you, we were the first to tell
you that, hey, paradigm C exists. The
entire distribution of probable economic
outcomes is shifted to the right. Here
are all the psychological and political
reasons why paradigm C exists. Here are
all the economic reasons why paradigm C
exists. And ultimately, here's how the
markets are likely to price in paradigm
C. And so, uh, you know, if we're
correcting this summer at some point,
uh, I just want you guys to
dispassionately execute our kiss and Dr.
Mo signals. And if you're not a 42 macro
client, you should be. But if you're

level of investing experience they have
in their goal to retire on time and
comfortably. So getting back into uh
this executive summary uh elsewhere uh
President Trump urged Fed chair Jerome
Pal to cut rates in their first meeting
since November 2019 reiterated that FOMC
policy will be objective, careful,
non-political and aligned with statutory
mandates of price stability and full
employment. In short, don't expect the
Fed to make headlines anytime soon. Last
but not least, tucked inside the 10,00
plus page One Big Beautiful Bill Act,
One Big Ugly Bill Act, is section 899,
which targets unfair foreign taxes.
Section 899 raises taxes on passive
income for holders of of 31 trillion in
US assets uh foreign holders of 31
trillion in US assets from
discriminatory countries. Investors are
concerned that the revenge tax could
deter foreign investment and erode US
exceptions. As we stressed throughout
the first half of April, Trump's trade
war had already devolved into a capital
war. Section 899 merely gives the
administration a tool to fight back.
Let's hope that they don't have to use
it at all or use it wisely if they do.
Because if they fail to wield it
properly, durable Fed yield curve
control will arrive far sooner than our
current by December 2027 expectation.
And why December 2027? That's three
congressional budget cycles from now.
We're currently, not currently, they're
currently um legislating the one big
ugly bill. Uh after that, they're
talking about fiscal 2026 midterms or
the fiscal 2026 budget, which ends in
September 30th of 2026. Next fall,
they'll be talking about the fiscal 2027
uh uh budget. And then the following
fall, they'll be legislating and
negotiating uh um the fiscal
2028 budget uh in in the fall of 2027.
And that that will be obviously with a
different Congress. Um, so investors
need to be aware
that in my personal opinion based on all
the research I've done on this topic and
I think I've done more research than
anybody on global Wall Street as an
individual in understanding the
geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market
that all of Wall Street consensus is now
waking up to this year. I don't think
the bond market's going to give us more
than three budget cycles to get this
right uh from a fiscal policy
perspective. So, uh, that's why we
expect yield curve to arrive by December
2027. So, uh, could be wrong on that.
Could be wrong on everything I say. So,
let's just keep moving. Uh, as always,
we'll transition to, uh, we'll wrap up

claiming that Trump will end up with
capital controls. Foreign investor

dividends and interest on US stocks and
some corporate bonds by 5 percentage
points every year for four years. It
would also impose taxes on American
portfolio holdings of sovereign wealth
funds which are currently exempt. Why
are they exempt? It's nuts. What's your
take on it? Given that many 42 macro
clients are not US citizens, will there
be an alternative? Again, yeah, there
will be an alternative. It's called
international markets. US exceptionalism
is a function of exorbitant privilege.
We're the only country in the world that
could capitalize our stock and bond
markets at these ridiculously low
interest rates or our low rates. If you
look at our earnings yield or our
10-year nominal treasury yield with an
11% twin deficit, the GDP ratio. So
yeah, this if they wield this section
899 authority improperly as deemed by
our foreign creditors and foreign
investors, they will take their capital
ball and go home. And that to me is part
and parcel with the geopolitically
driven supply demand imbalance in the US
Treasury bond market that we identified
years ago in the context of our
investing during a forturning regime uh
econometric analysis. And so, uh, I
definitely think this is a a big risk. I
don't I don't think we're going to come
out of the gate swinging, uh, but
certainly if if negotiations go poorly,
and they may very well this summer, um,
we're not expecting them to end poorly,
but the negotiations may proceed poorly.
We're expecting them to end very
positively because ultimately we think
the menu of policy options is narrowing
from the Trump administration to deliver
on their campaign promises, which is to
engineer golden age for working-class
American citizens. And so we think
they've pivoted from paradigm B, which
would have taken too long and caused too
much pain, at least more pain than they
were willing to tolerate. And ultimately
now they're transitioning to paradigm C,
which is, you know, let's just pour so
much water on this thing that the
trickle down turns into a a gushing down
of of of of money and jobs and and and
and income, real income growth for folks
on the bottom part of a paradigm AK that
been copy and pasted over. So uh you

on this. I think it's going to be that,

before we wrap up you know I just want

historically large share of the world's
capital and this is before Europe's
remilitarization kicks in Japan Japan's
monetary policy normalization is
completed and China's strategic
decoupling concludes each of these
global macro forces represents less
foreign demand for US Treasury debt
because it requires s capital for them.
It's either going to destroy capital in
the case of Japan's policy of
normalization. It's going to require
capital in the case of Europe's
remilitarization and it's and it's going
to stop the flow of incremental capital
in in terms of China strategic
decoupling. Like these are all things
that are removing big swads of demand
from the US Treasury market. These are
the world's three largest international
investment surplus economies. So if we
if we if if they need their own money
andor they're destroying their own money
and or they're no longer investing at
the same speed while we don't have the
